Environmental Impact Statement, Debris Removal, North Branch Chicago River, Illinois

The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers proposed a project to clear debris from a 12-mile stretch of the North Branch of the Chicago River in Illinois. The aim was to remove fallen trees, roots, and other debris that contribute to flooding, pollution, and unsightliness. The project was intended to improve the river's appearance and enhance recreational use, although it would not address major pollution sources. The community was involved, with local groups and agencies expressing support and concerns about environmental impacts.
North Branch Chicago River, Cook County, Illinois

The project to clear debris from the North Branch of the Chicago River was proposed to address flooding and improve the river's aesthetics and recreational potential. While there was community involvement, including support from local groups, there were also concerns about the environmental impacts of the debris removal. Ultimately, the project aimed to enhance the river's usability without tackling the underlying pollution issues.

"The North River Commission strongly favors this cleanup of the North Branch. Through local cleanup efforts in the past we have had limited success in removing accumulated debris." — North River Commission Environmental Statement

"Our primary concern relative to this cleanup project concerns protection of the live trees and vegetation growing along the river banks and on the river bed area which contribute to the sightliness of the Albany Park/North Park neighborhoods." — North River Commission Environmental Statement
